author: @tomsuderman9976
text:
I have an off-grid 400W solar kitchen assist system.  Our house is on grid energy.  I ran parallel wiring into kitchen so we can choose between grid or off-grid power by plugging into desired wall plug.  It sends solar energy into a 3000W inverter that is secondary power for our kitchen small appliances. It can power the large refrigerator/freezer during the day but the 400Ah battery bank is not quite enough to power frig through night. 
 Can a Supercap be used to inject power into the battery bank in early morning hours so energy in bank could hold up until sun shines to recharge system power?

author: @stinkycheese804
text:
Except it's all nonsense.  The issues were sustained load, and amount of drain.  The supercap does not reduce the load, nor does it reduce the drain.

One benefit would be high instantaneous current, but what do you plan to use that for? Perhaps starting a vehicle in extremely cold weather or spot welding.  Otherwise when you consider the series/parallel array of supercaps you'll need, it makes more sense to just do without them and use a larger lead acid battery instead, if not lithium depending the capabilities of the charging system.

In other words for most applications, given the same budget, having a lead acid battery with the array of supercaps in parallel to it, is inferior to spending same amount of money on a larger lead acid battery, which is why that is what practically every industrial use is designed for, and also vehicles and their starting batteries.

Most things simply aren't designed for extreme current because that in itself causes problems with needing to beef up everything else to withstand that much heat because we have not yet perfected and implemented superconductors in everything.

If all you really want is to reduce ripple enough that it's not a problem for the powered devices, a large lead acid battery is already low ripple but you could then improve it using standard, lower cost capacitors rather than supercaps, AND if talking volume or cost, you can deliver a higher current with low ESR capacitors than supercaps, so the supercaps really don't have a purpose except in niche applications.

author: @immrnoidall
text: Can someone help me make a supercap battery to run in parallel ,after my 8S, 24VDC 300Ah lifepo4 pack, so my inverter and solar charge controllers have a chance to shut down at 10.5vdc before one of the lifepo4 cells ,shuts the pack down above 10.5 vdc?  In theory. The 8s pack should make it that low, but it never does. Both the inverter and solar charge controller warn to "NEVER DISCONNECT BATTERY FIRST OR UNDER LOAD FIRST".  The supercap pack would not need to work long at all. It just needs to handle the high amps of charge/discharge, and to reach 10.5 vdc after lifepo4 shutdown.

author: @htannberg
text: It would be very interesting if Tesla would use a bank of super capacitors for taking all the power during regenerative breaking.  Then split their battery pack into two sections at 400 volts each. The capacitors would dump their energy into the lowest voltage battery section, then that section would rest while the other section powers the motors.  Both batteries would be used in parallel while under hard acceleration.   When charging the battery sections would be arranged in series for an 800 volt fast charge.  When driving everything is still configured for 400 volts.  I wonder if the extra effort would make financial sense.

author: @JaenFoster
text:
This is similar to an idea I had but at a much smaller scale.
The stability and efficiency of the grid relies on the supply and load being in near perfect balance. Too much supply and the grid voltage rises and things go pop. Too much load and the voltage sags and you get blackouts. One of the main problems is that the grid supply is slow to respond. It can take upwards of 30mins from the demand increasing to a power plant upping its output, so predicting when the load is going to increase is important. There's obvious times. 7am and people get up and stick the kettle on. (While one 1kVA load is insignificant, 10 million 1kVA loads on the other hand.) So here's the idea, have homes install "powerwalls" that can balance the demand at the load side, rather than the supply side. everyone gets up and sticks the kettle on, or there's an ad break just before a world cup final penalty shootout (so everyone gets up and grabs another beer from the fridge) and rather than having to predict exactly when the load surge is going to happen, the load side energy storage supplies the demand locally, and then slowly recharges over the course of the day while your out at work. Add in some smart metering so the supply knows how much has been used, and you can keep the grid in near perfect balance.
Probably explained that badly, but I'm sure you get the idea!

author: @Infinion
text:
I have a 16.8V 1000F (6s2p ,2.8V 3000F cells) that I use in my car with a small 10W solar panel. Because of the low internal resistance, I have been able to turn over the car at as low as 7V. Lowest I've got is 6V with a push start (it is standard, not automatic).
I have been wanting to use a DC-DC converter not to charge from a lead-acid, but to extract the useful stored energy below 6V. It's not easy finding a 200A rated 12V boost converter that can run down from 1V or lower. But in the off chance that there is no such product, I wonder if anyone would be interested in me designing a low voltage extremely high current boost converter specifically for supercapacitors and low voltage cells?

author: @4nrgy
text:
well, it's a bad idea.
1st: supercapacitors are quite expensive which makes more sense to just invest this money into more batteries.- this way we solve all the problems and have bigger storage too 😜. for 100Ah battery 20A discharge current is 0,2C which is suboptimal. for 2*100Ah (200Ah) 20A is 0,1C which is acceptable. 100Ah 12V battery is 50£ or 60€.
 and it will solve DoD problem as well. 1,2kWh from 100Ah 12V battery is 100%DoD. 1,2kWh from 2*100Ah 12V battery is only 50% DoD 😁
2nd: biggest sin in engineering: complicated design. DCDC inverter as a Single Point of Failure.  
3rd: this design of yours doesn't solve anything. energy stored in this capacitors is not enough for any real life scenario's apart from EV's(rapid acceleration)

author: @tsbrownie
text: Put a super-cap across a battery and you're got a (brief, but damaging) short circuit. Trust me on that.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: certainly - if you mismatch them - put a 2.7 volt supercap across a a 1.5 volt battery and all that will happen is you supercap will charge - put a 12 volts battery across  the same super capacitor and you will blow the supercpapacitor - plus what I suggested being done - hassocks been done - of course it was done by folks who knew not to mismatch the cap

replies.author: @tsbrownie
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I've put 2 x 2.7 volt supercaps in series across a 3.3v LiPo and melted leads, arced contacts, etc. The instantaneous current is something to be accounted for.

author: @VinoVeritas_
text: I'd suggest LTO chemistry cells instead of supercapacitors. They're much cheaper than supercapacitors at present and have much greater energy density. I recently bought a 552Wh (2.3V, 20Ah, 12S) battery for £110. I intend to use it for an instantaneous hot water tap (90°C) for making tea and coffee.

replies.author: @rolliebca
replies.text: Hi Vino, where did you get your LTO's from? Great chemistry choice for very cold climates like mine.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it would still work with LTOs mate - in supercapacitor terms every battery is a snail lol

author: @Sam3532
text: How would this affect the available run time though?

replies.author: @sumitmamoria
replies.text: Probably not much better, might even be worse due to all the power conversion losses. The main motivation seems to be to improve battery health rather than increased output energy

replies.author: @Sam3532
replies.text: @sumitmamoria no, sorry, I should have explained further. Surely the capacitors will have a limited storage; smaller than the bank of batteries, so the output from the capacitors, which can handle a higher output, will be limited - before your back to using the battery and should limit max output to save it from damage..? I guess on second thoughts it will run almost as long but will provide a buffer of protection for run times that don’t exceed the storage in the capacitors, before they recharge

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: not much - this is really peak shaving and to cope with high power demand over short periods of time - like starting a motor - the start of a. motor has a very high draw the running of a motor much much less - you are using the caps to be in the middle of that and they are tied to the battery bank - you second comment was pretty good by the way

author: @yoda-be.at1746
text: Peukerts law. All to do with internal resistance. Much less of a problem for lithium ion. I've not understood why capacitors haven't been combined with battery tec.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they. have - it could be you are a little behind mate

replies.author: @yoda-be.at1746
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering lol, maybe

author: @nitebirdscuffle4213
text: Thank you Bud =) .........Was really hoping you would address the battery to cap issue........Some lithium Ion batteries instantly failed in this kind of set up, actually over discharging to fill the cap.......

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lithium is notoriously delicate - a little control electronics is needed if using lithium - you can only really connect a lead acid straight up and even then it's not the best

author: @davidabineri908
text: Lots of words here but all this would be much more useful if the ideas were implemented with instruments to show the effect being discussed.  I still have yet to see (perhaps I missed it) a light bulb being lit up by any of the devices shown in these videos.

replies.author: @elizabethmckinney7182
replies.text:
Light bulbs are very poor examples of power.  They're really easily faked, require a wide variety of power input, and are very nonspecific as a meter. 

Robert regularly includes meter readings on all his generator videos. Which is how it should be done.

replies.author: @elizabethmckinney7182
replies.text: This particular video is more a "thought exercise" not a demonstration.  He will sometimes offer a demonstration later if necessary.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are lots of examples - you could try my suggestion and have a look on google scholar - but I suppose that would take you doing something - so why on earth would you do that?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate

author: @colleenforrest7936
text: Could you use that as a way to hook up power from different sources (solar and wind) to the same battery

replies.author: @MrBrelindm
replies.text:
Most of the larger integrated solar charge controllers nowadays have connections for the battery bank (including individual cell monitoring), a three phase AC inverter (up to 25KW continuous output, and the ability to seamlessly charge from solar, wind, and even shore power or automotive or any combination of those at the same time. 

The Chinese have had them for years now. They are the innovators in this field. You can buy them online but many aren't yet UL listed so caveat emptor applies. But due to tariffs and shipping inflation they aren't as inexpensive as at first blush they appear. However, it remains that the technology is quite well developed already by the Chinese and there's a thriving domestic market for them.

replies.author: @MrBrelindm
replies.text: They're often described as a "solar inverter" when searching for them online.

replies.author: @MrBrelindm
replies.text: They are programmable with different power profiles. So they can easily be reconfigured for a battery upgrade - even if the cell chemistry used changes. They are quite versatile.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes - several systems have been researched to do exactly that - have a quick look on google scholar with hybrid battery supercapacitor micro generation as key words
